content.full_text:
Re:
Fern Por-rrrcAr- Pnecrrcss C oMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 6f0 r Sacramento. CA 9-581.+-2329
(916) 322--5660 o Fax (9t6) 322-0886
February 19, 2008
John P. Fraser
5417 Rolling Rock Road
Placerville, CA95667
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-011
Dear Mr. Fraser:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Nothing in this letter should
be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesblt
(1975) I FPPC Ops.71.)
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
May you make or participate in making a decision whether to allocate additional
water supplies to the Shingle Springs Rancheria (the "Rancheria") when your home is
located approximately one-half mile from the Rancheria?
CONCLUSION
You may make or pa(icipate in making the decision whether to allocate water to
the Rancheria so long as there are no indications that the decision may affect the final
size or capacity of the casino cr.rrrently under construction.
I The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections ti 1000 through g l0l4. All
statutory references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair
Ptrlitical Practices Commission are contained in Sections I tl I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
,5i;


File No. A-08-011
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are an elected director on the board of the El Dorado Irrigation District (the
"EID"). The EID is a public water supplier in El Dorado County. Also within El Dorado
County, you and your wife own your home in the Grassy Run subdivision. Your home is
located on one of the 89 five-acre parcels within the subdivision. Located immediately to
the west of the subdivision is the Rancheria. The Rancheria is located approximately
one-half mile from your home.
Currently, the Rancheria is building a casino on its land. As part of its casino
plan, the Rancheria is attempting to secure water from the EID. You have sought advice
regarding your participation in the decision whether to allocate water to the Rancheria.
In previous advice issued regarding your participation in a similar decision, we concluded
that you could not vote because specific circumstances existed making it reasonably
foreseeable that having a casino built adjacent to your property would have a material
financial effect on your economic interest. (Fraser Advice Letter, No. 4-02-054.)
However, you have asked for reconsideration of our previous advice in light of
additional facts indicating that the casino will be completed regardless of the EID's
decision whether to allocate water to the Rancheria. Specifically, you have provided the
following facts:
ln environmental studies for the casino and for the highway interchange to
provide access to the Rancheria, the Rancheria has declared its intention to
build the casino whether or not the EID provides water to the casino.
a
If the EID does not provide water, the Rancheria intends to import water by
tanker truck and store the water in one or more on-site tanks.
The construction of the casino, the highway interchange, on-site water storage
tanks, and on-site wastewater treatment and disposal systems commenced in
early 2007 and is progressing at a rapid rate.
The Rancheria has announced that all construction will be completed and the
casino will be open for business in October 2008.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The
a
a
o


File No. A-08-011
Page No. 3
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(l)-(S).)
Step One: Are you a "public official?"
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency...."
(Section 82048.) As a member of the EID's board of directors, you are a public official
within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation fi7}2J.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the govemmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) As a member of the EID's board of directors, you are making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision when considering the
allocation of available water supplies by the water district.
Step Three: What are your "economic interests?"
Of the economic interests recognized under the Act, those interests implicated by
your account of the facts are the following:
Real Property -- A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
Regulation 18703.2.) Presumably, you have an investment interest of $2,000 or more in
your residence in the Grassy Run Subdivision. Accordingly, you have an economic
interest in this property.
Personal Financial Effects -- A public official always has an economic interest in
his or her personal finances. In particular, a government decision has a personal financial
effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,


File No. 4-08-011
Page No.4
assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation
18703.5.) 
2
Step Four: Is your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following
apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part
of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
govemmental decision. . ..
"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property....
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in
which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."
2 We point out that public officials always have an economic interest in their personal finances.
However, any tinancial effect the decisions could have on your residence is considered to be an efTect on
your real property interest and would not be analyzed separately under the "personal frnancial eff'ects"
rules. (Regulation 18705.5(a).) Accordingly, the personal financial effects rule does not appear to apply to
your circumstances and we will not discuss it further.


File No. 4-08-011
Page No. 5
Because your residence is not located within 500 feet of the property and you
have not provided any facts that the governmental decision involves any of the types of
decisions outlined in Regulation 18704.2(aX2)-(6), it appears that your economic interest
in your residence is only indirectly involved in the governmental decision.
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interest?
Materiality
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
For properties indirectly involved in a govemmental decision, Regulation
18705.2(b)(l) provides that the financial effect is presumed not to be material. However,
this presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding
the govemmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in
which the public official has an economic interest that make it reasonably foreseeable the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the official has
an interest. Examples of these specific circumstances include circumstances where the
decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation


File No. .{-08-011
Page No. 6
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (t975) I Fppc ops. 19g.)
Based upon the facts you have provided, the governmental decision at issue is
whether additional water supplies will be provided to the Rancheria. In prior advice to
you regarding a similar governmental decision, we determined that there were specific
circumstances that made it reasonably foreseeable that the EID's decision will have a
material effect on your property despite the fact that your property was only indirectly
involved in the govemmental decision. At the time, it appeared that the determination of
whether to provide the additional water supplies could have affected the Rancheria's
ability to build a casino on the property adjacent to the subdivision in which your
property is located. Accordingly, it was foreseeable that the determination could have
affected the character of the neighborhood in which your property is located including,
but not limited to, substantial effects on the "'view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels,
air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood,' as well as lights, crime levels, and
property values." (Fraser Advice Letter, supra.)
Notwithstanding our prior determination, you have now provided the additional
facts that the Rancheria intends to secure its water supply from an alternative source (if
the EID elects not to allocate water to the Rancheria); that the construction of the
highway interchange, the casino, the Rancheria's on site water storage tanks (necessary if
the Rancheria has to secure water from an alternative source), and on-site waste water
treatment and disposal systems commenced in early 2007 and is progressing rapidly; and
that the Rancheria has announced that the casino will be open for business in October of
this year. In light of these additional facts, the EID's decision whether to allocate water
to the Rancheria will not prevent the Rancheria's from completing the casino project.
Barring additional facts indicating that the EID's decision may affect the final size
or capacity of the casino, it does not appear that the decision will affect the character of
neighborhood if the casino will be completed regardless of the EID's final determination.
Accordingly, the financial effect on your economic interest in property is presumed not to
be material, and you may make or participate in making the decision to allocate water to
the Rancheria. Note, however, that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in
providing advice (ln re oglesby, supra), and you are ultimately responsible for
determining whether the EID's decision may affect the final size or capacity of the casino
currently under construction.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 1S707(a).)


File No. A-08-01I
Page No. 7
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision under the "legally required participation" exception despite the fact
that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest. This exieption applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the govemment agency would be paialyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to your ciriumstances, so we
will not address them further.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
v:
G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGLjgl

govemmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that

a

2

Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an

govemmental decision. . ..

annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property....
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in
which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the

or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which the official

economic interest;

18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (t975) I Fppc ops. 19g.)
Based upon the facts you have provided, the governmental decision at issue is

water supplies to the Shingle Springs Rancheria (the "Rancheria") when your home is
located approximately one-half mile from the Rancheria?

CONCLUSION: You may make or pa(icipate in making the decision whether to allocate water to
the Rancheria so long as there are no indications that the decision may affect the final

Your home is located on one of the 89 five-acre parcels within the subdivision. Located immediately to the west of the subdivision is the Rancheria. The Rancheria is located approximately one-half mile from your home. Currently, the Rancheria is building a casino on its land. As part of its casino plan, the Rancheria is attempting to secure water from the EID. You have sought advice regarding your participation in the decision whether to allocate water to the Rancheria. In previous advice issued regarding your participation in a similar decision, we concluded that you could not vote because specific circumstances existed making it reasonably foreseeable that having a casino built adjacent to your property would have a material financial effect on your economic interest.
